finding: `test11_mac_transport_verified` is dead code that never compiles on any target. The enclosing `unsupported` module is declared in mod.rs under `#[cfg(not(any(windows, target_os = "macos")))]`, but this test is gated `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]`. The two cfgs are mutually exclusive, so on macOS the whole module is excluded and on non-mac hosts the test's cfg is false â€” the assertion (and its `super::super::macos::MAC_TRANSPORT_VERIFIED` reference) is compiled out everywhere. It gives the appearance of verifying the DEC-9 mac-transport gate but provides zero coverage (the real, live copy is `macos.rs::tests::mac_transport_verified`).

install_cert_trust stages the CA at a predictable, non-exclusive path `temp_dir()/ziee-bridge-cert-<pid>.cer` via `std::fs::write` (follows symlinks, no O_EXCL), then feeds it to a privileged `security add-trusted-cert -d -r trustRoot`. When TMPDIR is unset (common for launchd/daemon contexts) temp_dir() is world-writable `/tmp`. A local attacker who pre-creates a symlink at that path or swaps the file's bytes between the write and the `security` invocation (TOCTOU, CWE-377) causes an attacker-chosen certificate to be installed as a trusted root â€” a trust-store compromise. Use an O_EXCL/mkstemp-style unique file with restrictive permissions in a per-user dir.

install_cert_trust writes the CA to a predictable path `temp_dir()/ziee-bridge-cert-<pid>.cer` with `std::fs::write` (no exclusive-create), then `run_elevated_certutil` runs `certutil -addstore -f Root "<path>"` under an elevated (runas/UAC) process, installing into the machine-wide LocalMachine\Root store. A same-user, non-elevated process that swaps the file contents between the write and certutil's read (TOCTOU, CWE-377 â€” the path/pid is guessable) gets an attacker-chosen CA into the machine root store, crossing the userâ†’admin trust boundary. Stage the cert with an exclusive-create unique name and ACL it before invoking certutil.

The no-secret-leak guarantee is asserted only as a case-insensitive substring scan of the whole body for "token"/"secret". It is a weak guard: a leaked secret whose field name/value contains neither literal substring would pass. The adjacent explicit allowlist of the four permitted fields (enabled/port/last_connected_at/cert_fingerprint) is the real protection; the substring checks add little. Not a defect in behavior, just low-signal assertions.

run_office_js_real_llm_declares_mode is a real-LLM network test but, unlike every sibling live/real-LLM test in this file, it is NOT marked #[ignore] (it only soft-skips when ZIEE_OFFICE_REAL_LLM_URL is unset). In any environment where that env var happens to be set, this test fires a live LLM HTTP call during the default `cargo test` run and can fail/hang on model non-determinism, whereas the other real-LLM tests are opt-in. Inconsistent gating for an otherwise identical class of test.

Comment says office_bridge::use is 'granted to the Users group in migration 133', but in this desktop crate the grant lives in the sibling file 10000000000007. The referenced 'migration 133' (and 'migration 97/41' on lines 9-10) are server-crate numbers that do not exist in this crate's migration set â€” a stale cross-reference left over from the serverâ†’desktop relocation. Harmless to execution; misleads a maintainer tracing the perm grant.

The RAISE WARNING text hardcodes 'migration 133:' as its self-identifying prefix, but this file is migration 10000000000007 in the desktop crate. If the Users group lookup fails, the emitted operator warning points at a nonexistent migration number, making the diagnostic harder to trace back to this file. The grant logic itself is correct and idempotent.

stage_cert_der: guard built after create_dir â†’ temp dir orphaned on open/write error; deterministic pid+counter name could EEXIST-DoS after crash+restart. (Caught by phase-7 re-audit; fixed: RAII-from-creation + nanos nonce.)
